title: MCP HttpTransport accepts any URL with no SSRF or scheme validation
description:
## Severity
Critical
## Dimension
security
## Location
src/mcp.rs:284-293 (HttpTransport::new), src/mcp.rs:635-640 (McpManager::connect_http)
## Violation
`HttpTransport::new` accepts an arbitrary `&str` as the MCP server base URL and trims trailing slashes; it never parses the URL, never enforces a scheme allowlist, and never invokes the SSRF guard from `src/web.rs::validate_url`. `McpManager::connect_http` forwards it directly.

```rust
pub fn new(base_url: &str) -> Self {
    Self {
        client: reqwest::Client::new(),
        base_url: base_url.trim_end_matches('/').to_string(),
        request_id: AtomicU64::new(1),
    }
}
```

A user (or compromised config) can register `http://169.254.169.254/`, `http://localhost:8080/admin`, `file:///...` (rejected by reqwest, but inconsistent), or `http://metadata.google.internal/` — and every subsequent MCP tool call will reach that endpoint with attacker-controlled JSON-RPC bodies. The `connect_http` path is the primary trust boundary for remote tool servers and currently has no perimeter defense.
## Mandated refactor
In `HttpTransport::new`, parse with `url::Url::parse` (return `Result<Self, McpError>`), enforce a scheme allowlist (`http`/`https`), and invoke the same SSRF guard used by the web tools — either by lifting `validate_url` into a shared `net::guard` module or by injecting a `UrlValidator` trait. Update `connect_http` to propagate the validation error.
## Filed from QA review dispatch
